Reports show four fatalities and 17 injuries. Kimberly, her husband Sam, and their 18-year-old son survived but were hurt. Kimberly and her son Alex were released from the hospital that day, though they still suffered severe injuries. Kimberly received a huge hematoma on her hip, while Alex suffered from three different breaks in his lumbar spine. Sam suffered traumatic brain injury, a possible broken neck, multiple broken ribs, a lung contusion, and a possible spine injury. He was released from the hospital three days later. The banana truck driver was hospitalized for several days with severe burns. As of now, news reports list no formal charges against him. However, several civil cases are underway against the driver, his company Co-Lease Co Inc, DTE Energy, the Michigan Department of Transportation, Michigan State Police, and even individual officers seen in this video. News reports indicate civil suits blame the driver and Co-Lease Co Inc for negligence. One attorney claims DTE Energy neglected to hire a contractor for traffic control when installing the power lines, using state troopers instead. Because of this, there were allegedly no warning signs for traffic or construction down the road. On top of that, the permit was set to begin on November 3rd. The crash happened 18 minutes before midnight on November 2nd. A DTE Energy spokesperson said the power line project was scheduled at night to limit travel impact and that they work closely with M. and state police. They added, they cannot place traffic signs or billboards on M. roads like I-96. Aaron Jenkins, an M. spokesperson, can be quoted saying, this was a DTE project. They had permits for the work. It is not an M. work zone, but the Michigan State Police was providing traffic control. MSP should be able to tell you all about that. A victim's attorney has been quoted saying, there's still no explanation why they were using MSP to do this. No explanation why they were not using standard uniform temporary traffic control, which would have avoided this whole situation. It's easy to blame the semi-driver, but he wouldn't have been put in that situation if they had done this correctly from the start. News articles say state police blamed the inattentive truck driver, yet no charges are reported. In police reports, the driver claims a sudden plume of engine smoke filled his cab, blocked his view, and caused the crash. A witness driver told police the semi swerved approximately six miles prior to the crash. Fearful of their driving, the witness passed them in the passing lane before later getting involved in the accident. When passing by, they saw computer screens in the cab and noted there was no smoke coming from the truck. That witness said the semi never fully left their lane but seemed drunk or distracted and was driving fast for a semi. The report also indicates a blood sample was taken from the semi-truck driver and no alcohol was detected. News sources say Co-Lease Co's insurer will pay $1 million, the maximum policy limit, to the 11 civil suits, citing the quote, understood facts and consequences of the crash. Those who have passed away include 29-year-old Robert Ross, who was most likely the individual ejected from his vehicle and off the overpass. The other three victims, a brother, sister, and the sister's daughter, were allegedly found under the burning semi. 47-year-old Brandon Glasco, 43-year-old Arthuriya Watkins, and 20-year-old Manessa Naval. Their family has been outspoken about the crash, sharing with news sources their frustrations with the situation.
